coredata.dc:description: Zinc-based batteries offer the advantages of low cost and high specific capacity. However, current research on zinc-ion batteries remains in the experimental period due to the uneven deposition of zinc ions during battery cycling. In this study, we prepared sp2-dominated partially carbonized nano-dots (PCDs) as an electrolyte additive to enhance the reversibility of the Zn anode. This work confirm that the Zn-philic functional groups enriched in PCDs facilitate the capture of Zn2+ ions in the electrolyte, thereby reducing parasitic side reactions involving water molecules. More importantly, it was discovered that the sp2-dominated structure of PCDs can be modified on the surface of the anode through π-electron interactions, regulating the inner Helmholtz plane (IHP) for uniform migration of Zn2+ ions. The absorbed inner molecular layer effectively inhibits dendrite formation and enhances zinc reduction kinetics. Both Zn symmetrical cells and Zn-MnO2 batteries demonstrate that incorporating PCDs significantly optimizes interfacial processes and extends both half-cell and full-cell lifetimes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the electrolysis process and material synthesis of PCDs; (b) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of PCDs; (c) Raman spectrum of PCDs; (d) C K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) of PCDs; (e) Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) of PCDs; (f, g) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of PCDs1(f) PCDs2(g) in C 1s, O 1s, N 1s region; (h) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of PCDs1; (i) Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) of PCDs1; (j) 3D Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images of PCDs1.

(a) FT-IR spectrums of three kinds of electrolytes; (b) Raman spectrums of three kinds of electrolytes; (c) XPS of PCDs combined with ZnSO4 in Zn region; (d) Kβ-edge XES of three different electrolytes; (e) X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) of Zn in different electrolyte; (f) Fourier transform extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) in R-space; (g-i) Wavelet transform of Zn K-edge: (g) ZnSO4, (h) ZnSO4+PCDs1, (i) ZnSO4+PCDs2.

(a) CV curve of Zn||Cu battery; (b) Tafel curve of zinc negative electrode in three electrolytes; (c) Coulombic efficiency (CE) of Zn||Cu battery; (d) Cycling performance of Zn||Zn symmetric battery at current density of 1 mA·cm−2, with a capacity of 1 mA·h·cm−2; (e) Depth of discharge is 50 % Zn||Zn battery with 10 um zinc foil.

(a) XRD pattern of the electrode in the Zn||Zn battery after 1000 cycles; (b)XRD intensity rate of different crystal planes. XPS spectrum of Zn||Zn after 1000 cycles: (c) X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) of Zn electrodes after 1000 cycles in different electrolytes; (d)XPS pattern in O-1 s region; (e) XPS pattern in O-1 s region Zn-2p; (f) XPS patterns under different depths in the Zn-2p energy region; SEM images of zinc deposition in Zn||Cu batteries in different electrolytes: (g) ZnSO4, (h) ZnSO4+PCDs1, (i) ZnSO4+PCDs2.

In-situ electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance mass dissipation curves of the negative electrode evolution in different electrolytes:(a) ZnSO4,(d) ZnSO4+PCDs1; AFM morphology of the cycled electrodes:(b) ZnSO4,(e) ZnSO4+PCDs1; 3D AFM morphology of the cycled electrodes:(c) ZnSO4,(f) ZnSO4+PCDs1; In-situ electrochemical impedance of Zn symmetrical cell and their corresponding DRT analyzing with different electrolyte systems(g1) ZnSO4, (g2) ZnSO4+PCDs1,(g3) ZnSO4+PCDs2; (h)Schematic diagram for interface structure of electrolyte within PCDs before and after cycling.

(a) In-situ XRD Zn||α-MnO2 at 0.1 A·g-1; (b) In-situ XRD contour plots of different crystal planes of Zn||α-MnO2; CV data of Zn||α-MnO2 at different scan rates:(c) ZnSO4,(d) ZnSO4+PCDs1; (e) Rate capability tested from 1 A·g−1 to 5 A·g−1; (f) The cell capability decay trend from 1 A·g−1 to 5 A·g−1 under the electrolyte ZnSO4+PCDs1; (g) Electrochemical performance of Zn||α-MnO2 button cells with different electrolytes; (h) Cyclic stability test at 1 A·g−1.

head.ce:title: Regulating inner Helmholtz plane by partial carbonization nano-dots additive for reversible zinc metal anode

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc-based batteries offer the advantages of low cost and high specific capacity. However, current research on zinc-ion batteries remains in the experimental period due to the uneven deposition of zinc ions during battery cycling. In this study, we prepared sp2-dominated partially carbonized nano-dots (PCDs) as an electrolyte additive to enhance the reversibility of the Zn anode. This work confirm that the Zn-philic functional groups enriched in PCDs facilitate the capture of Zn2+ ions in the electrolyte, thereby reducing parasitic side reactions involving water molecules. More importantly, it was discovered that the sp2-dominated structure of PCDs can be modified on the surface of the anode through π-electron interactions, regulating the inner Helmholtz plane (IHP) for uniform migration of Zn2+ ions. The absorbed inner molecular layer effectively inhibits dendrite formation and enhances zinc reduction kinetics. Both Zn symmetrical cells and Zn-MnO2 batteries demonstrate that incorporating PCDs significantly optimizes interfacial processes and extends both half-cell and full-cell lifetimes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc-based batteries have the advantages of low price and high specific capacity (820 mA·h·g−1) [1–3]. The electrochemical reaction of zinc-based batteries involves the transfer of two electrons, which provide aqueous zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs) higher volume energy density and has better application prospects [4,5]. However, under current conditions, the research on ZIBs is still in experimental stage. Main reason is that the uneven deposition of zinc during the battery cycle will cause serious dendrite effects. Even worse, charging and discharging process are also accompanied by the problem of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in the acidic electrolyte [6–8]. In order to solve these shortcomings, various methods have been developed to protect zinc metal anodes and optimize the battery system, such as zinc anode structural modification, electrolyte optimization and artificial interface layer (SEI) design [9]. Recent studies found that electric double layer (EDL) is the one of the key factors impacting the interfacial properties, which closely related to dendrite growth and hydrogen evolution. Our previous work also revealed that it is the local field around anode plays the main role to regulate anode electrochemical process [10,11].

The direct way to tuning EDL on anode is to modify anode surface with artificial SEI. However, long-term zinc stripping/plating result in surface state unstable. Construction of mixture electrolyte and modify solvent structure of Zn2+ can help establish stable EDL on anode continuously [12]. For examples, newly-induced high polarity ligand can replace water molecule and combined with Zn2+ forming a new solvent structure to avoid the parasitic reaction from water. Wu et al. used the strong interaction between the CO group in trifluoroacetamide (TFA) and Zn2+ to change the structure of Zn(H2O)6 2+. At the same time, TFA destroyed the hydrogen bond network in H2O, resulting in the reduction of H2O and the stabilization of the pH of the electrolyte, thereby improving the deposition kinetics of Zn2+ [13]. Besides, additives can directly act on the surface of zinc metal to form a stable protective layer to induce uniform deposition of zinc metal. Li et al. prepared a multifunctional universal electrolyte by adding dextran (linear sugar) to Zn(CF3SO3)2, which can also reduce the energy barrier of [Zn(H2O)6]2+ desolvation and selectively adsorbed on the Zn(002) surface to effectively provide desolvated Zn2+ for Zn deposition, the adsorbed dextran layer also homogenizes the electric field at the electrolyte interface, allowing for uniform zinc deposition [14]. Recent research reports show that electrolyte additives can also be used to adjust the electric double layer structure to achieve protection of the metal anode [15]. The SEI ability of the electric double layer (EDL) regulator gives the Zn surface a uniform and dense SEI layer, further enhancing the Zn-philic and hydrophobic components of SEI, thereby mitigating parasitic reactions and the formation of zinc dendrites. Huang et al. used 0.5 vol% sulfoxide as an additive to control the nucleation process on the zinc surface, promote the uniform distribution of the SEI layer, and enhance the reversibility and stability of zinc anode plating/stripping [16]. Weng et al. used 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium trifluoromethanesulfonate ([EMIM]OTF) as a system additive to change the deposition orientation of Zn2+, the ion shielding effect exhibited by OTF- effectively reduced the HER caused by H2O molecules [17]. However, a new problem is that during the long cycle of the battery, these reactive additives are continuously consumed, resulting in the attenuation of battery performance. Thus, avoiding side reaction between additives and Zn and designing stable additives is meaningful for improvement of AZIBs [18]. Compared with traditional molecular additive, surface modified Nano-dots exhibit more stability in polar electrolyte. Zhang et al. introduced hydrophilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into the electrolyte and used the binding effect of GQDs with Zn2+ to form a dendrite-free and strong zinc anode. It has been confirmed that surface functional group can largely enhance the adsorption and participate co-plating process to regulate electrochemical performance. Due to due to limited characterization techniques, further reaction mechanism is still in infancy for nanodots. So, it is of great significance to design active nano-dots and find out the impact from the stable and soluble additive.

In the current work, a sp2 dominated partial carbonized nano-dots (PCDs) has been obtained through modified electrochemical methods by tuning ratio of urea and sodium citrate. It was found that high concentration of urea can accelerate the polymerization of citric acid and improve the formation of sp2 bonds in the as-formed products. To control the voltage and oxidation time, the products can maintain a form of partial carbonized nano-dots. Compared with regular carbon nano-dots, PCDs on one hand display stronger functions with Zn2+ in the solution. The properties of Zn-philic function groups can simultaneously control the formation of thermodynamically stable cores and inhibit side reactions. On the other hand, sp2 bonds dominated carbon skeleton benefit for the formation of the EDL structure, which effectively inhibiting zinc dendrite growth during the reaction process. In-situ DRT analyzing reveals that under the function of the electric field, the PCDs adsorbing Zn2+ will be closely arranged on the zinc anode, thus forming a thermodynamically stable layer (Inner Helmholtz plane, IHP). The IHP can regulate the electric field distribution on the Zn surface as well as the deposition/stripping of zinc. In addition, it increases the nucleation points, thus refining the Zn nucleation particles in the early stage of the reaction and making the zinc deposition denser. In this work, with the addition of PCDs, the reversibility and coulombic efficiency (CE) of the zinc anode are significantly improved, and the cycle stability of the Zn||Zn symmetric cell extend to over 2300 h at 1.0 mA·cm−2. High depth of discharge (DOD) (50 %) can also be obtained due to the regulation of IHP through inducing sp2 PCDs. It is believed that this work not only provide a new additive to ZIBs but also reveal the impact of IHP around anode, which may inspire a new concept of interfacial design of metal anode.

PCDs was prepared through a simple electrochemical oxidation method. First of all, 1.47 g sodium citrate dihydrate and 0.9 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water (DI). The concentrations of both substances are 0.5 mol·L-1. Two symmetrical Pt foil were used as electrolyte with area of 1 cm2 and the distance of two electrolyte was 1 cm. The electrochemical oxidation was carried out on constant voltage power supply. The potential was controlled to 5 V and maintain 6 h to prepare the final PCDs1. The obtained brown solution was then dialyzed for 2 h for purification. Final concentration of PCDs is around 9.59 mol·L-1. Electrolyte was prepared by adding DI water and ZnSO4·7H2O directly to PCDs solution. Volume was controlled to 15 mL and Zn2+ concentration was controlled to 2 M. To regulate oxidation reaction, the molar ratio of sodium citrate dihydrate and urea has also been changed from 1:3 to 2:2. The control sample was marked as PCDs2. The as-prepared electrolyte was optimized and controlled the PCDs concentration of 6.8 mg·mL−1.

The crystallographic information of the as-prepared products was established by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/max-2500/PC, Rigaku) using Cu Kα radiation. In-situ XRD was also measured on the above instrument. α-MnO2 was loaded onto carbon paper as the cathode and assembled into an in-situ XRD electrochemical cell for constant current charge–discharge testing. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, ThermoFisher Scientific) was utilized to characterize the valence state of elements. The microstructure and morphology of the Zn deposits were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-5500, Hitachi). The microstructure of the PCDs were examined by transmission electron microscopy (Talos F200X G2, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the prepared PCDs were measured using a spectrofluorimeter (F-4600, Hitachi) in ambient air at 25 °C. The AFM images of the electrodes were obtained using an atomic force microscope (MFP-3D Infinity, Asylum Research) produced by Oxford, UK. The electrochemical diagrams and frequency-dissipation curves were obtained using a quartz crystal microbalance (AWS A-20). A Ti-Au-plated quartz chips (fundamental frequency 10 MHz) was used as work electrolyte. X-ray emission spectroscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy of various electrolytes and zinc foil were measured on the easyXAFS 300+. Soft X-ray absorption spectroscopy was collected at 4B7B beamline in Beijing synchrotron radiation Facility (BSRF) with TEY mode, Beijing, China. Nuclear magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR)were obtained on a Bruker 500 M instrument using D2O as solvent. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurements were achieved using a Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS50 and ATR mode. The liquid sample was collected into a capillary tube and the Raman spectrum (LabRAM HR spectrometer, Horiba) was measured under a 514 nm light source signal. The experimental results were normalized by OH bonds at 3000 cm−1–3500 cm−1 after background subtraction to compare the intensity.

Linear polarization curves (LSV) were performed in a three-electrode structure, in which zinc foil as the work electrode, copper foil as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was acquired from an electrochemical workstation (Solartron 1470E, AMETEK) with a signal amplitude of 10 mA and frequency range of 10-1-105 Hz. The electrochemical properties of Zn metal in ZnSO4, ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 electrolytes were tested using Zn||Zn symmetrical cell. Galvanostatic charge/discharge was performed on Neware (CT-ZWJ-4′S-1U, CT-4008T) under current densities of 1.0 mA·cm−2 and 2.5 mA·cm−2, respectively. The capacity is 1.0 mA·h·cm−2 at 1.0 mA·cm−2 and 2.5 mA·h·cm−2 at 2.5 mA·cm−2. Coulombic efficiency was measured by assembling Zn||Cu coin cell. And the depth of discharge (DOD) measurement was also measured by using Zn||Cu coin cell. Here the thickness of Zn foil is 10 um. In the current work, Zn plating capacity is controlled to 2.94 mA·h·cm−2 to ensure DOD reach 50 % for cycling. The electrolyte loading mass of each battery is 100 ul and the zinc foil is 16 mm in diameter. Glassfiber (Watersman GF/D No.1823-125) separators are used to separate the cathode and anode.

Zn-MnO2 full cell are assembled to evaluate the practical performance. 20 um 99.9 % Zn foil is used as anode and MnO2 is used as cathode materials. The cathode is prepared by mixing MnO2 powder, Superp and PVDF in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) at a mass ratio of 7:2:1, and then coat it on high-purity titanium foil (Ti 99.9 %). The loading mass of cathode is about 1.0 mg·cm−2. CV is measured ranging from 0.8 V to 1.8 V with different scan rate of 0.1 mv·s−1; 0.2 mv·s−1; 0.5 mv·s−1; 1 mv·s−1. Dapp and kex are cucullated based on the Randles-Sevcik and Dahms-Ruff equations through the CV result. Energy Density is calculated based on the total mass of cathode. In-situ EIS is measured on Autolab PGSTAT302N. Battery was first galvanostatic discharge to a potential state and keep the final voltage to measure EIS.

A modified electrochemical strategy (Fig. 1 (a)) was developed to prepare PCDs. Firstly, sodium citrate dihydrate and urea were mixed into deionized water and stirred to make the liquid transparent. A voltage of 5 V has been applied between two Pt foil electrolytes. During this process, the two raw materials dissociated and reorganized on Pt electrode. Final sample was obtained through electrochemical carbonation reaction. When sodium citrate dihydrate and urea interacted with each other, the hydrogen bond network constructed between them was very complex, mainly because both sodium citrate dihydrate and urea showed amphiphilicity in the property of forming hydrogen bonds [19,20]. Based on previous reports, citric acid will finally change into the derivative of piperazine citrate [21]. To learn the structure features of as prepared PCDs, FT-IR were firstly carried out. As shown in Fig. 1(b), for both two of PCDs, the broad absorption peaks between 2700 and 3600 cm−1 prove the presence of OH and NH stretching vibrations. The strong signals at 1674 and 1394 cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CN stretching modes, respectively, and the peak at 1077 cm−1 is assigned to the CN stretching mode. The two sharp peaks at 1077 and 838 cm−1 originate from the CN and NH stretching modes respectively. According to previous reports, these function groups are similar to the properties of citrazinic acid [21]. At the same time, it can be found that some functional groups are the same as the raw materials. That mean the PCDs are not totally carbonized, there are still some NH2, COOH left. According to previous report, these active functional groups show strong interaction with Zn2+ and can impact the Zn-O coordination in the electrolyte [22]. Fig. 1(c) shows Raman spectrum of PCDs. First of all, the typical D-band and G-band located at 1365 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 clearly confirmed the formation of carbon [23]. The broaden and shift of peaks also reflect a low degree of carbonization. From Raman result, it can also be concluded that PCDs1 contain more carbon with sp2 structure defect due to its higher ID/IG. Further nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) exhibits more details of the structure and functional groups. More peaks can be observed than expected in C13-NMR. As shown in Fig. S1, in the chemical shift of 155–187 ppm, the C structure mainly exists in the form of COOH, and the resonance peaks in this region indicate the formation of various carboxylic acid functional groups [24]. The proportion of PCDs2 under the resonance peak here is higher and the chemical shift is at a higher position (163.47 ppm). The reason is that the electron density around carbon changes, and the conjugation effect will cause uneven electron distribution, which will lead to changes in the carbon chemical shift [25,26]. This indicates that the C structures in the two substances are not exactly the same, which may lead to differences in electrochemical and other performance tests [24]. The results are well agreed with the previous Raman, and they are also confirmed by soft-XAS shown in Fig. 1(d) [27]. Comparing the XAS of the two materials, it can be found that in PCDs1, carbon exhibit the hybridization state of sp2 and sp3. While, in PCDs2, only peaks represent the sp3 statement can be found. Previous researches shows that more urea can effectively promote the carbonation process, causing a high ratio of sp2 [28,29]. The existence of urea will induce a large number of unsaturated bonds which lead to a significant increase in the number of π bonds. The mass ratio of urea in PCDs2 is small, so the unsaturated bond structure generated is relatively scarce. This characteristic directly leads to a low degree of carbonation of the material, which in turn causes a significant reduction in the activity of the material. The special sp2-dominateed structure makes it easy to confined on anode surface to construct a new EDL further tunning Zn plating.

Due to the unsaturated bond, both two samples exhibit the potential of fluorescence. Fig. 1(e) is the PL of PCDs. In this work, the wavelength of the excitation light was chosen 450 nm. Two curves were obtained. The absorption peak position of PCDs2 was blue-shifted relative to PCDs1. According to literature reports, the oxidation of groups on the surface of the material will drive the blue shift of PL [30,31]. Consistent with the results of XAS, the carbon structure of PCDs2 lacks sp2 hybridization and its oxidation degree is lower than that of PCDs1.So, PCDs2 exhibit less C-O unsaturated bond. Fig. 1(f, g) are the XPS patterns of PCDs, which can provide a detail analyzing on chemical states. By analyzing the C1s spectrum of PCDs1, the first peak at 284.7 eV can be attributed to the CC bond, the two peaks at 285.5 and 286.8 eV in the middle are attributed to CC and CN/CO covalent bonds, respectively, and the peak at 288.5 eV is attributed to CN/CO. Quantitative analysis of carbon also shows that the ratio of unsaturated carbon in PCDs1 is 78 %. A detailed analysis of the C 1s of the two materials revealed that the PCDs2 sample had fewer unsaturated bonds. The XPS spectrum in N1s region shows that nitrogen mainly exists in the form of pyrrolic nitrogen and amidogen. The XPS spectrum of O1s reflects that the O element has binding Energy at 531.7 eV, 532.1 eV, 533.3 eV, and 535.9 eV, respectively, which are attributed to the four structures of CO, CO, COH, and OH. [19,32] Analysis of the XPS results for PCDs2 reflect a similar structure but with less unsaturated bonds as we discussed before. Combined the above result, it can be concluded that both PCDs exhibit an enriched active group which have the potential to chelate Zn2+ in electrolyte. Differently, carbon in PCDs1 tend to exhibit a sp2 state due to the addition of unsaturated raw. To observe the as-prepared PCDs, TEM and AFM is used to further measure the size. From the AFM results of Fig. 1(i, j), it was found that PCDs seems prepared as a shape of nano-sheet pieces. The size of PCDs was less than 5 nm in vertical dimension and about 10 ∼ 20 nm in horizontal scale. It should be noted that due to the property of active groups in PCDs, it is easy to agglomerate. So, the result from AFM may larger than the real. Similar results can be also obtained from TEM images in Fig. 1(h). The yellow circle marked some isolated carbon dots while the red marked some large agglomerate PCDs.

Taking the bare electrolyte (2 M ZnSO4) as the reference, a series of characterizations of electrolyte were carried out to observe the combination of PCDs and Zn2+ in electrolyte. First of all, molecular vibration spectrum was used to compare the solvent structure. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), FTIR shows the structures of the three electrolytes, reflecting the main functional groups in the SO and OH stretching modes. Similar shapes of vibration peaks exhibit that PCDs shows no obvious impact on H-bond solvent network. Fig. 2(b) is the normalized Raman spectrum (Raman). An obvious vibration around 980 cm−1 cans be attributed to the vibration of free SO4 2- in the electrolyte. The addition of PCDs makes the intensity of the vibration increased. The main reason may due to the interaction between Zn2+ and PCDs. PCDs adsorbs Zn2+ to the surroundings and replace the SO4 2-. More SO4 2- dissolved in the electrolyte and change free. This guess comes from the structure features of PCDs. As we mentioned above, PCDs is enriched with NH2 and COOH. According to previous conclusion, [33] Zn2+ in the solution possess an unfilled s/p-electron orbit, which allows them to form strong chemical adsorption bonds with O/N that have unpaired electrons. The strong interaction between Zn and N/O can replace original SO4 2-. XPS in Fig. 2(c) in Zn 2p region provide direct evidence. As shown in the figure, after combined with PCDs, the binding energy of Zn exhibit a slight shift to lower energy, which means the electron tend to transform from Zn to N/O. Moreover, compared with bare ZnSO4, the larger energy shift also shows that PCDs can partially replace H2O and SO4 2- chemically combining with Zn2+.

From another aspect, we observed the local environmental of Zn2+ to reveal the role of adding PCDs. X-ray emission and absorption spectroscopy (XES and XAS) can provide more elemental information and local coordination information. [34,35] In the samples ZnSO4+PCDs, emission energy of Zn-Kα is around 8637.2 eV (Fig. S2), which moves toward a lower energy level compared with bare ZnSO4. In the obtained XES-Kβ spectrum (Fig. 2(d)), ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 show emission energies of 9569.9 eV and 9570.1 eV, respectively, which are lower than 9570.6 eV of ZnSO4. The shift of XES-Kβ is mainly attributed to the introduction of a portion of NH2 to coordinate with Zn. This shows that the average oxidation state of Zn in ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 is lower than that in ZnSO4, because the oxygen in PCDs replaces the oxygen in SO4 2- to form a coordination structure with Zn2+. The addition of PCDs changes the chemical environment of zinc ions resulting in shifts of the ZnO binding energy, giving atoms different oxidation states.

Fig. 2(e) shows the XAFS of different electrolytes. It can be concluded based on the position of the main-edge(E0) that the modified Zn maintains the same +2 valence state as in the bare electrolyte. However, through compare the XANES, different absorption shape reflects a distorted ZnO coordination structure. In the bare electrolyte, it can be assumed that all six O around Zn2+ are the same. In the newly formed electrolyte, both N from NH2 and O from H2O/PCDs can coordinate with Zn2+ which may result a distortion of Zn(O/N)6. Further structure evolution can be analyzed from the extend fine structure (EXAFS) as shown in Fig. 2(f), a comparison of the Fourier transform data in R space, which provides the solvation structure of Zn2+ in the first shell. From the pattern, the obvious shell near R = 1.58 Å represents Zn-O coordination. For the ZnSO4+PCDs1 fine structure in the figure, the decrease in intensity indicates that the addition of PCDs will weaken the solvent effect of water and reduce the coordination between water and Zn2+. The slight leftward shift of the first shell in R-space reflects the shortening of the Zn-O bond length. The reduced disorder of ZnSO4+PCDs2 may reflect the interaction between O and Zn. Fig. 2(g, h, i) Wavelet transformed (WT) EXAFS further elucidates the local structure of Zn2+.Compared with bare ZnSO4 electrolyte, the strongest coordination center of ZnSO4+PCDs1 electrolyte moves to a lower k value. The addition of PCDs not only shortens the length of the Zn-O bond, but also shifts the first coordination center to a lower k value, indicating that PCDs successfully optimizes the coordination structure of Zn2+. Six water molecular were replace by other atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of PCDs modified electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: Above results clearly exhibit that by adding PCDs, coordination statement of Zn2+ can be largely adjusted. Strong interaction between Zn2+ and NH2/COO can partially replace water. Based on previous report, [36] that may tune the decomposing pathway during discharge process. In addition, we also infer that the highly sp2 ratio of PCDs can also help construct EDL on anode. To further find out the property of electrolyte after adding PCDs, contact angles (CA) and other electrochemical methos were used. The result of CA (Fig. S3) shows that the electrolyte containing PCDs has poor wettability to the metal anode. Then pH effect from PCDs was evaluated. It was found that after adding PCDs, pH of electrolyte increased from 4.2 to 5.7 and 5.65 for PCDs1 and PCDs2. This slightly changes result from the appearance of NH2 and other N contained alkaline groups. To adjust the concentration of PCDs, it was found that pH is stable around 5.7, which means PCDs can also act as buffer solution to stabilize acidity of electrolyte.

The properties of the electrolyte directly affect the cycle life of the battery. First of all, a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method was performed. A stronger redox peak appeared in the CV curve of the Zn||Cu cell in Fig. 3 (a). Higher current density around −0.2 V and 0.2 V confirms that the PCDs additive enhances the reaction kinetics [37]. The corrosion potential of Zn in different electrolytes is confirmed by the Tafel curve as displayed in Fig. 3(b). As shown in the figure, the potential of bare electrolyte is −0.98 V, while the potentials of PCDs modified electrolyte are −0.97 V and −0.96 V respectively. In Table S1, we also listed the fitted corrosion potential and corrosion current, which shows that after adding PCDs, the corrosion current will decrease representing for an improved anticorrosion performance. This means that after PCDs modification, the stability of the metal under the influence of the electrolyte is improved. Compared with the zinc anode in the bare electrolyte, that in the PCDs modified electrolyte is less susceptible to corrosion. Controlling the nucleation of zinc ions is of great significance for the practical application of rechargeable ZIBs. Through data analysis of the overpotential of Zn||Zn symmetric batteries, it can be found that the addition of PCDs (Fig. S4) increases the overpotential to 69.7 mV and 72.2 mV respectively. According to the classical nucleation theory, a larger overpotential can not only reduce the nucleation radius, but also increase the nucleation rate and density, which is beneficial to the formation of thermodynamically stable and uniformly denser nucleus [38].

The utilization of zinc during symmetrical battery cycling is critical to battery life. To evaluate the potential for long cycling performance, columbic efficiency was measured in Zn||Cu battery. 10 mA·h·cm−2 Zn metal was firstly decomposed on Cu substrate as a zinc reservoir, and then the cycling performance was tested under 1 mA·h·cm−2 until voltage increased to the highest cut-off [39]. As calculated in Fig. 3(c), after adding PCDs, the utilization rate of zinc has been improved. Compared with the bare electrolyte, CE of PCDs can reach 99.16 %, which means excellent performance. Further electrochemical performance has also been evaluated by observe the life of Zn||Zn symmetric batteries. As shown in Fig. S5, under 1 mA·cm−2, battery with ZnSO4 as the electrolyte is less than 160 h, while the cycle life of the battery with PCDs added reaches 2345 h (Fig. 3(d)) and 2036 h (Fig. S6), respectively. At a higher current density (2.5 mA·cm−2), the Zn||Zn symmetric battery can also achieve a cycle life of 1163 h and 1024 h without a significant increase in voltage (Fig. S7). Further, we have also compared this performance to the reported literature with different carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dot (GQDs) in Table S2. It can be summarized that both GQDs and PCDs posse abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and have a strong binding effect with Zn2+, which can reduce the nucleation energy barrier and decrease the nucleation overpotential, and thus realize a dendrite-free zinc anode. Compared to regular GQDs, PCDs plays multiple roles and obtained a better performance.

In order to reflect the optimization of the uniformity of zinc deposition, a deeper depth of discharge (DOD) was performed on the Zn||Zn symmetrical battery. 10 um high-purity zinc foil was used for a 50 % depth of measurement. The DOD test cycle life of the blank group is only 144 h (Fig. S8) and the ZnSO4+PCDs1 group and ZnSO4+PCDs2 group can achieve long cycle lives of 1008 h (Fig. 3(e)) and 501 h respectively (Fig. S9). These excellent cycle lives and low overpotential clearly exhibit that the adding of PCDs can largely improve the plating/striping process.

It can be inferred that the addition of PCDs can inhibit the side reactions and tuning the plating of zinc on anode from the macroscale. After long time cycling measurement, anode was analyzed in detail from the aspect of surface morphology and surface valance. Fig. 4 (a) is the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of the Zn anode after 1000 cycles. Characteristic peaks of by-products appeared on the surface of the cycled zinc anode. By comparing the literature and the standard curve, we determined that it was Zn4SO4(OH)·nH2O(ZHS) [40]. According to literature reports, this substance is a common by-product of the anode in ZIBs. It can be found that the characteristic peaks of the zinc anode by-products after the PCDs addition reaction are weakened. As shown in Fig. 4(b), after the introduction of PCDs, the crystal plane orientation of the zinc electrode changes. It can form a preferential orientation of the Zn (100) crystal face, thereby inhibiting the directional growth of Zn (002). According to previous literature reports, Zn2+ tends to induce a single nucleation point on the top of the Zn (100) layer. In the electrochemical process, the Zn (100) crystal orientation is conducive to the formation of more active sites and ion current concentration [41]. In order to further understand the surface of the zinc anode, XAFS K-edge information of the same anode was recorded as shown in Fig. 4(c).The K-edge white-line peak of the ZnSO4+PCDs1 group and the ZnSO4+PCDs2 group has a energy shift, which proves that the oxidation state of the zinc electrode is reduced, the zinc electrode is better protected. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to get a better insight into the deposition/stripping process. As shown in the Fig. 4 (d,e), the composition of the zinc surface after 1000 cycles was analyzed. Among them, in the O 1s spectrum of the surface in Fig. 4(d), two regions can be assigned to ZnO (531.7 eV) and OH (534.2 eV) [42]. These can be attributed to the production of Zn(OH)2·nH2O. In the O 1s spectrum in ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2, the ZnO group was detected at a binding energy of 531.7 eV, which can be attributed to the signal of O in the combination of COOH and Zn in PCDs. Corresponding to the Zn 2p spectrum, the signal of the by-product Zn(OH)2 (1023.7 eV) only appears in the ZnSO4 group. The zinc signals in ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 are mainly composed of ZnO (1021.7 eV) bonds [43]. To further explain the reaction process, the zinc electrode pieces were etched in different depths. By comparing the Zn 2p spectrum after 1 min and 4 min etching (Fig. 4(f)), the Zn(OH)2 peak area ratio in the Zn 2p spectrum decreased significantly, and the Zn-O peak area ratio increased, indicating that the addition of PCDs limits side reaction between water molecules and zinc. The interaction between ions, the surface SEI layer composed of PCDs and zinc ions prevents electrode corrosion and hydrogen evolution. In the ZnSO4+PCDs1 group, the peak position of Zn 2P will shift with the change of etching depth. Whether it is Zn 2P1/2 or Zn 2P3/2, after 1 min of etching, the peak value shifts to lower energy, which may be related to the SEI layer formed on the surface. Increasing the etching depth will shift the binding energy of the Zn-O bond to a higher energy, and also proves that the coordination environment of the Zn-O bond changes [44]. The above XPS data confirms that the combination between Zn/Zn2+ and functional groups affects the transport of Zn2+ ions. During the Zn deposition process, changes in the interface layer play a crucial regulatory role [45].

Fig. 4 (g,h,i) shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Zn deposited onto Cu (1 mA·h·cm−2). A detailed result of anode plating can be further observed. Fig. 4(g) reflects the metallic zinc negative electrode in the blank electrolyte. It can be seen that large crystal grains with a size of micron are produced. The experimental groups whose electrolytes are ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 do not have corresponding large crystal particles, and the protective layer formed on the surface remains flat. It can be found that for the electrode test after cycling, all the results can be well confirmed.

From the above results, we inferred that PCDs can adsorb on surface of anode through π actions and affect electrochemical performances. In order to verify the conclusion, some measurement has been carried out to further study on microscopic interfacial dynamics during electrochemical process. Firstly, in-situ EQCM with dissipation was employed to monitor the reversible mass evolution. The cathode current collector is a 12 mm diameter Ti/Au-plated quartz wafer (10 MHz), the anode is high-purity zinc foil. During the test, to guarantee the reaction on cathode complete, a rest step has been inserted between each charge/discharge. A small current density is firstly used for activations, and then large current density is used for mass monitoring. Fig. S10 shows the frequency/dissipation changes during the activation process. According to the Sauerbry equation, there is a linear relationship between the frequency of the quartz crystal and its mass. Therefore, by calculating the frequency change of the quartz crystal, the change in the amount of metal deposited on the electrode can be obtained. The experimental results shown in Fig. S11 show that reversible mass efficiency in the ZnSO4 electrolyte is 89.62 %, while that in the ZnSO4+PCDs1 electrolyte is 93.67 %, which was calculated through the frequency changes during charge/discharge. It can be seen that after the introduction of PCDs, the extraction/insertion rate of zinc ions is higher and the reversibility of the mass change is better. Part of the Zn2+ loss mainly may come from the formation of SEI.

In order to further study the dynamic changes of the entire deposition process, we performed a detailed process analysis of the time–frequency curves. During the discharge process, mass change in ZnSO4 electrolyte is always linearly related to the charge change. In contrast, mass change in ZnSO4+PCDs1 electrolyte is not well linearly related to the charge state changes. During the testing process, we measured the dissipation(D) from EQCM, which can not only well reflect the viscoelasticity of the film, but also reflect the structural evolution of the film [46,47]. Fig. S12 provide the whole activation processes and their mass changes. The final frequency is far lower than the initial may due to the formation of SEI. Fig. 5 (a, d) provide the result from a high current density measurement. Genarally, for a rigid surface film, as mass increases, the frequency will decrease and as thickness increases, the dissipation will increase. But for non-rigid film, the changing trends of frequency and dissipation are not strictly related. In the electrolyte of bare ZnSO4, though frequency increase tends to be linear, the dissipation is different. After 5900 s, the dissipation shows a sudden increased indicating a formation of roughness surface and surface porous structure. However, in the ZnSO4+PCDs1 electrolyte, frequency increase still exhibits a linear decrease. Changes of dissipation experienced two stages. From 5800 s to 5850 s, dissipation keep stable indicating formation of dense layer. This is related to the adsorption of PCDs on the surface of as-formed Zn on cathode, which may reconstruct the IHP with PCDs and hydrated Zn2+. Afterwards, a relatively dense (high-density) interface layer is gradually formed on the surface due to the strong interaction between Zn2+ and O/N groups on PCDs. According to the literature, the dense structure can buffer the changes in electrode volume, thereby maintaining the relative stability of the structure [48]. The formed SEI layer can regulate ion transport, guide the uniform deposition of zinc and suppress side reactions, and improve cycle stability [49].

From the frequency and dissipation changing during the rest process, more dynamic issues can be concluded. The frequency slightly decreases in bare ZnSO4 may reflect that surface deposition lags behind the electron process. Due to the affect from PCDs, Zn deposition in ZnSO4+PCDs1 follows a quite different dynamic process. PCDs will combine with Zn2+ to construct the IHP and the absorbed more anions. Under the voltage drive, more anions and cations will be pressed to IHP. So, during discharge process, the mass increased quickly. With no voltage condition, anions and cations fall off from the local surroundings of Zn. The mass will decrease gradually. Similarly, a sudden fade of dissipation also provides IHP is no-grid layer on Zn. From the atomic force microscope image (AFM) in Fig. 5(b,c,e,f), it can be found that the average height of the samples in the ZnSO4+PCDs1 is 0.8 um, and the average height of the samples in the ZnSO4 is 1.2 um. More Zn-philic groups can induce a uniform and fine Zn deposition. The particle size in the image confirms that PCDs refines the size of Zn crystal. For further demonstration, 3D surface topography was also used to observe the surface layer after cycling. Fig. S13 provides the results, reflecting the average surface roughness of the cycled anode. Compared with the ZnSO4 electrode, the ZnSO4+PCDs1 electrode exhibits a roughness of 1.008 um which is smaller than in ZnSO4 electrode (1.052 um). It is consistent with our previous conclusion. It can be seen that under the conditions of introducing PCDs, the extraction/insertion rate of zinc ions is higher and the reversibility of the mass change is better. Part of the Zn2+ loss mainly comes from the formation of surface SEI. This is consistent with the results reported in the literature. According to the literature, the flake structure parallel to the electrode plane is denser than the block structure inclined or even perpendicular to the electrode surface, which not only reduces the formation of dead zinc, but also improves corrosion resistance. So, sp2-dominated PCDs can induce oriented deposition.

In this work, in-situ impedance method has been developed to detect interface evolution during battery testing. In order to further clarify the interface ion transfer during the electrochemical process, we performed relaxation time analysis (DRT) on the impedance spectrum to visually display the changes in the electrochemical reaction under different electrochemical states [50,51]. The DRT method can separate the electrode reaction process through time constants and more accurately and quickly reflect the dynamic process evolution of the electrode interface. The results are shown in Fig. 5(g), where the abscissa represents the test frequency.

Fig. 5(g1) shows the changes of interfacial dynamics during the charge and discharge process in ZnSO4 electrolyte. The obvious peak marked in red at high frequency (above 10000 Hz) is related to by-product layer, which keep no changes during the whole electrochemical process. Based on previous researches, the maximum peaks between 2000 Hz and 100 Hz reflect the resistance of the double electric layer relaxation process before the reaction begins. With the first discharge, the entire impedance is divided into three processes. The maximum value in the mid-frequency region is the impedance of the electric double layer relaxation process (τ1). The intensity of the entire process decreases and shifts to the low-frequency region. The two electrochemical processes at high-frequency to medium-frequency range are related to the interface impedance of the original metallic zinc (τ2) and the metallic zinc interface impedance of the deposited layer (τ3). Due to the Zn2+ plating and dissolving, τ1 exhibit an obvious shift to reflect the structure changes of EDL. The first cycle is active process. After the first cycle, peak shapes get broaden. Especially at junction of charge and discharge, τ1 divided into much small peaks and shifted to mid-frequency region as marked with blue in g1. The main reason is that when balance of solvation/desolvation destroy, movement of ions will be affected by the relaxation time and will not change in time. It will cause a short-term interface separation between charges and ions. From Fig. 5(g1), we can also conclude that at the begging of recharge process, τ1 will experience a large evolution due to the process of Zn dissolving and solvation, which may due to the sluggish dynamics of inertial reaction.

Fig. 5(g2) and Fig. 5(g3) are the in-situ EIS of ZnSO4+PCDs1 and ZnSO4+PCDs2 respectively. In the initial state, PCDs and Zn2+ are combined and adsorbed on the zinc surface to form IHP, which will reduce side reactions on Zn. So, no obvious peaks around 10000 Hz can be detected. Due to the participation of PCDs, the resistance of the double electric layer moves to low frequency. The two peaks in the mid-frequency region between 100 Hz and 1000 Hz are the electric double layer (τ1) and the surface protective film (τ2). It also means the increase of electrical double-layer capacitor. So, this provides evidence that PCDs can absorbed on Zn surface to regulate IHP. Compared with g1, it can be found that junction of charge and discharge, the complex electrochemical process caused by the separation of ions and charges was still observed. But it only divided into two peaks, indicating a regulated interfacial process. As battery in ZnSO4 electrolyte, the first cycle is an active process. Obvious changes occurred on the second charge/discharge process. During the second discharge process, stable position of τ1 shows an enhanced desolvation process. Compared with ZnSO4 electrolyte, the adding of PCDs can prevent the weaken and split of τ1. This means newly formed IHP by PCDs can improve desolvation dynamic process through tuning interfacial electric field. Also, it plays a role of buffer to balance solvation/desolvation and weaken the affect from Zn2+ and solvate. Combined with the DRT results and EQCM-D observation, a schematic diagram have been summarized in Fig. 5(h). Under the action of the electric field, the closely arranged thermodynamically stable layer (IHP) formed by the PCDs can accelerate the solvation/desolvation dynamic and regulates the electric field distribution on the zinc surface and improves the uniformity of zinc deposition/stripping.

To evaluate the potential of PCDs added electrolyte, Zn-MnO2 full cell was assembled. α-MnO2 is selected as the cathode material in the experiment and the results are displayed in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) provide the phase evolution of α-MnO2 during the first discharge/charge cycle through in-situ XRD. Obvious changes can be observed in the contour plot (Fig. 6b). It can be observed that a reversable phase of ZnSO4·3Zn(OH)2·5H2O (ZS) appears during discharge process, which may due to the insertion of H+ and Zn2+ into the hybrid cathode. [49] The increase of OH– leads to the participation of ZS in the ZnSO4 electrolyte. In the subsequent charging process, it can be seen from the in-situ XRD images that ZS gradually disappears during the charging process. The enhanced frequency response and kinetic performance have also been verified by using in-situ EIS under different potential. Fig. S14 shows the Bode plot of EIS. During discharge process, the phase degree peak above 45° tend to shift to high frequency indicating an improved kinetics after adding PCDs in electrolyte. To further study the function of PCDs modified electrolyte, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed. [52–54] To further confirm the role of MnO2, Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) was employed. Fig. 6(c) and (d) exhibit the CV of batteries under different scan rate. The main oxidation peaks around 1.6 V is related to the insert of cations. Under low rate, both of them are similar. while during the discharge process, battery with PCDs shows a changed reduction process at 1.1 V–1.4 V, which means the addition of PCDs can absorbed on cathode through π function to impact the reaction kinetics [4]. From CV curve, we further investigate the reaction kinetics through calculating the quantitatively apparent diffusion coefficient (Dapp ) and homogeneous electron self-exchange rate constant (kex ). In current work, Dapp and kex are cucullated based on the Randles-Sevcik and Dahms-Ruff equations. Dapp are calculated to be 1.34*10-10 – 3.11*10-9 cm2 s−1, and kex are quantified to be 4.55*107-1.06*109 M−1 s−1. Compared with typical charge exchange process (10-13-10-10), [55,56] PCDs1 added electrolyte possesses a remarkable rate capability and faster electron exchange capability. Fig. 6(e)-(h) and Fig. S15 provide the battery performance under different rates. Under current density of 1 A·g−1, the initial charge capacity can reach 206 mA·h·g−1 and 255 mA·h·g−1 for ZnSO4 and PCDs. As the current density increased to 5 A·g−1, battery without PCDs will experience serious capacity fading. Compared with it, battery with PCDs can still reach 137.7 mA·h·g−1 even under high current density. To evaluate the practice application potential of current full battery systems, energy density and practical energy output have been provided in Table S3. As shown in table, battery with PCDs electrolyte exhibits large energy density of 218.06 Wh·kg−1 which is due to the high operating voltage and high capacity. Under the same condition, bare ZnSO4 electrolyte can only provide 203.39 Wh·kg−1. From Fig. S16 it can also be concluded that over the entire power range, full battery with PCDs1 can deliver excellent energy density. Even current density reaches to high of 5 A·g−1, the power density is 4620 W·kg−1 with energy density of 124.74 Wh·kg−1. Moreover, the advantage of adding PCDs can also be confirmed from the long-term cycling performance under 1A·g−1. As shown in Fig. 6(g) and (h), battery with PCDs in electrolyte can not only exhibit higher initial capacity but also shows an excellent capacity maintenance. After 300 cycles, the capacity can still reach 111.2 mA·h·g−1. While for the ZnSO4 battery, after 300 cycles, the capacity can only maintain 53 mA·h·g−1. It means that the addition of additives limits the capacity decay trend of the cell. From the above results, it can be concluded that PCDs shows large potential to improve the performance of Zn-MnO2 battery through tuning the stability of anode.

In the current work, a simple electrochemical method was used to prepare sp2 dominated partial carbonized nano-dots (PCDs) by using urea and sodium citrate. X-ray based structure analyzing shows that the as-prepared PCDs exhibit two features: one is enriched surface N/O contained unsaturated functional groups, the other is the sp2 hybridization dominated carbon skeleton. Further experiment shows the former makes PCDs can chelate Zn2+ and impact the dissolvent process, while the later will help PCDs absorbed on anode forming a new EDL structure. Also, in-situ DRT and EQCM revealed that under the action of the electric field, the PCDs adsorbing Zn2+ will be closely arranged on the zinc anode, thus forming a thermodynamically stable layer (Inner Helmholtz plane, IHP). The IHP can further regulate the electric field distribution on the Zn surface and the deposition/stripping of zinc. In addition, it increases the nucleation points, thus refining the Zn nucleation particles in the early stage of the reaction and making the zinc deposition denser. With the addition of PCDs, the reversibility and coulombic efficiency (CE) of the zinc anode are significantly improved to 99.6 %, and the cycle stability of the Zn||Zn symmetric cell extend to over 2300 h at 1.0 mA·cm−2. High depth of discharge (DOD) (50 %) can also be obtained even under high current density. It is believed that this work not only provide a new additive to AZIBs but also reveal the impact of IHP around anode, which may inspire a new concept of interfacial design of metal anode.
